schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch map the chapter's two halves onto 2 Samuel 5 and 23 and recall David's road from Ziklag to Hebron; Ellicott reads 'all Israel' and the 'bone and flesh' proverb as marking the whole nation's covenant with its divinely chosen kinsman-king.
Matthew Henry reads the coronation as God's counsel ripening at last, and the covenant-oath as David's willing submission to rule by law.
Clarke refers the history to the fuller account in 2 Samuel 5.

themes: the covenant of king and people

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch make the verse the pivot toward the capture of Zion; Ellicott unfolds 'in time past,' 'leddest out and broughtest in,' and above all 'feed/shepherd my people' as the immemorial language of kingship, the king as God's representative shepherd (Rom. 13:1).

themes: the word of the LORD by Samuel

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the elders' covenant as Israel's constitution — the Senate determining 'the manner of the kingdom' before the LORD, ratifying the word already spoken through Samuel; free choice and sovereign decree prove to be one.
Ellicott traces the Chronicler's gloss 'which is Jebus,' the threefold name Jerusalem/Ursalimmu/Hierosolyma, and 'inhabitants of the land' as the standing name of the aboriginal Canaanites.

themes: the name of the holy city

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch set the capture of Zion in its parallel to 2 Samuel 5 and open the register of heroes; Ellicott fills out the Jebusites' jeer — the blind and lame would keep David out — and their trust in an impregnable fort.
Ellicott shows the Chronicler following a clearer account than Samuel's obscure 'waterfall/blind and lame,' making Joab an Othniel at Jebus; 'went up' means he scaled the rampart.
Matthew Henry reads David's offer as fired by opposition and the prospect of preferment — 'take away honour out of the soldier's eye and you cut off the spurs from his heels.'
Wesley notes the exact promotion: Joab, before a chief captain, is now made captain-general of all Israel and Judah.
Ellicott parses 'castle' as the rare masculine form (a mountain-summit citadel, cf. Arabic), and 'city' as acropolis; the stronghold takes its conqueror's name.
Matthew Henry lifts the naming into prophecy: the city of David is the 'holy hill of Zion' on which God sets his king (Ps. 2:6).

Ellicott reads 'from Millo round about' as David fortifying the whole circuit (Samuel views it from wall inward); Keil and Delitzsch gloss Joab's work — the verb 'to make alive' (cf. Neh. 4:2) means rebuilding the ruined quarters.

Keil and Delitzsch note the remark opens the transition to David's reign; Ellicott expounds 'the LORD of hosts' — supremacy over the starry armies of heaven, hence over all earthly hosts, so David's greatness is credited wholly to God.

themes: the God of the starry armies

schools.summary: Matthew Henry meditates on David's worthies — strength borrowed from servants yet wrought by God; in strengthening David they strengthened themselves; their exploits show God's power (Josh. 23:10), and an Ammonite and Moabite among them foretoken Gentile worthies of the Son of David.
Ellicott renders 'the heads of the warriors' who strengthened David to make him king, per the word of the LORD (v.3), both for his own sake and Israel's (Ps. 78:70-72).
Wesley: David ascribed his success not to the hosts he had but to the LORD of hosts.

Keil and Delitzsch read 'chief of the thirty'; Ellicott treats 'Jashobeam' as possibly disguising Eshbaal, reads the feat as a spear brandished over 300 slain (cf. Shamgar), and inclines to Samuel's larger number as original.

Clarke brings the Targum's portrait and Kennicott's structural analysis (37 warriors in captain, two triads, and thirty; ~34 words lost from Chronicles); Wesley harmonizes 300 by his own hand plus 500 by others to Samuel's 800.

debates.question: How is the '300 slain' of Chronicles reconciled with the '800' of 2 Samuel 23:8?

sides.position: Samuel's larger number is likely original; the Chronicler's 300 an error, since the lesser Abishai slew 300
Jashobeam slew 300 by his own hand and 500 more fell to others who joined him, making Samuel's 800 — both true

schools.summary: Ellicott traces 'Dodo/Dodai' and the 'uncle' rendering, marks the Ahohite clan (perhaps Benjamite), and fixes Eleazar as the second of the first triad — his exploit about to be recovered from the lost lines.

themes: an exploit about to be restored

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies Pas-dammim (where David met Goliath), shows several lines lost by a copyist's eye skipping between two 'the Philistines gathered,' and restores Eleazar's stand from 2 Samuel 23; flags the barley/lentils textual crux.
Clarke, via Kennicott, lays Chronicles beside Samuel to restore the ~34 lost words of the exploit.

Ellicott reads the verbs as originally singular (Shammah's lone stand), pluralized by a later editor after the omission; 'saved them' differs from Samuel's 'made a great deliverance' by one Hebrew letter, the victory laid to the LORD.

Keil and Delitzsch note this exploit belongs to three unnamed men of the thirty; Ellicott weighs 'to the rock' against Samuel's 'at harvest,' places the cave of Adullam and the fertile, giant-named valley of Rephaim south-west of Jerusalem.
Keil and Delitzsch frame the coming deed of the three mightiest and the turn to the second class; Ellicott fixes 'the hold' as Adullam and the Philistine 'garrison' as an outpost holding Bethlehem.
Ellicott notes the well 'at the gate' can no longer be certainly located, the modern 'David's well' lying north-east of the town.
Clarke refers to the fuller treatment at 2 Samuel 23:15-17.
Matthew Henry names the longing an indulgent, homesick 'foolish fancy,' its interest lying in the self-denial David will presently show.

themes: the lost site of the well

schools.summary: Ellicott: the three broke through the outpost, not the main army; David 'poured it out' as a libation (cf. Gen. 35:14), a free sacrifice of thankfulness, not by prescription.
Wesley: the water too precious for his own drinking David poured out to the LORD — let God be honoured with the best.
Matthew Henry reckons the act among the greatest in the chapter — devotion toward God, offering him the best.

themes: water poured out to the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott explains David's counting the water as blood ('the life is the blood,' Gen. 9:4), notes the doubled 'their lives,' and reads 'these things did the three mighty men.'
Wesley: it threw David into confusion that brave men should risk their lives for water — great men must not be prodigal of their servants' blood.
Matthew Henry finds repentance for a rash longing and tenderness toward his men — the water turned, in David's account, to blood.

Keil and Delitzsch open the second class (Abishai and Benaiah); Ellicott gives the shorter form 'Abshai,' the missing 'son of Zeruiah,' and reads 'chief of the three' as head of the second triad, his 300-slaying feat matching Jashobeam's.

themes: Abishai chief of the second Three

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch render 'doubly honoured' and survey the roll's structure (37 in Samuel, sixteen added in Chronicles); Ellicott instead reads 'most honoured of the second triad,' the 'in the two' pointing to a second group of three.
Wesley: 'attained not' simply means he did not equal the first three.

debates.question: What does 'more honourable than the two' say of Abishai's honour?

sides.position: He was 'doubly honoured,' pre-eminent over his two fellows as their captain
He was the most honoured member of the second triad, distinguished within his rank

themes: the two triads and the Thirty

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch place Benaiah at the head of the second pair, above the thirty but below the first three, referring his deeds to 2 Samuel 23:20-23.
Clarke brings the Targum's expansive, plainly legendary portrait of Benaiah as a sin-fearing, law-reading saint, witnessing the awe of later tradition.
Clarke points to the fuller note on the parallel at 2 Samuel 23:21.
Ellicott renders 'five in the cubit,' judges 'weaver's beam' perhaps borrowed from the Goliath story, and distinguishes Benaiah's staff from David's — the two duels alike in fact, not by imitation.

Ellicott fixes 'a name among the three' as the second triad, Abishai's group; Keil and Delitzsch keep Benaiah in that graded place, above the thirty but below the first three — the summary a precise entry, not loose praise.
Ellicott reads 'above the thirty,' notes the omitted third member of the triad, and identifies 'his guard' (literally 'his obedience') as the Cherethites and Pelethites, David's foreign bodyguard.
Clarke gives the Targum's paraphrase, 'chief ruler over his disciples.'

Ellicott identifies the whole list as the famous band of the Thirty (Asahel 'among the thirty,' 2 Sam. 23:24), notes the pairing pattern and Judah/Benjamin concentration, and wonders whether Elhanan son of Dodo is another alias of Goliath's slayer.

Ellicott corrects 'Harorite' to 'Harodite,' notes the lost companion Elika, weighs 'Pelonite' against Samuel's 'Paltite,' and explains 'peloni' ('so-and-so') as perhaps a scribe's stopgap for an illegible name.
Ellicott places Ira at Tekoa in Judah and Abiezer at Anathoth in Benjamin, both reappearing as captains of David's monthly courses (1 Chr. 27) — champions who were also commanders.
Ellicott judges 'Sibbecai' correct (against Samuel's 'Mebunnai') and recalls his slaying of the giant Saph; sets 'Ilai' beside Samuel's possibly-truer 'Zalmon'; a Zarhite of Hushah in Judah.

Ellicott places both at Netophah near Bethlehem, Maharai a Zarhite captain of a monthly course; the second name appears as Heled, Heleb, and Heldai across the records — fragility and resilience of the text together.
Ellicott distinguishes this Ithai of Gibeah from Ittai the Gittite, and this Benaiah the Pirathonite from Benaiah son of Jehoiada; a Benjamite and, rarely, an Ephraimite of Pirathon.

Ellicott prefers 'Hurai' to 'Hiddai' (d/r confusion), places the brooks of Gaash in the Ephraimite hills, and traces 'Abiel/Abi-albon' to a possible original 'Abi-baal,' the idol-name later avoided; homes now unidentified.
Ellicott ties Azmaveth to Bahurim (Shimei's town in Benjamin) and Eliahba to Shaalbim in Dan, and renders 'Eliahba' as 'God hideth' — a warrior's name folded with faith.

Ellicott reconstructs 'Jashen the Gizonite' (an accidental 'sons'), and weighs 'Jonathan son of Shage the Hararite' against Samuel's 'Shammah,' owning what remains obscure in a badly worn pair of names.
Ellicott prefers 'Sacar' (wages) to 'Sharar' and gathers the Greek and Syriac forms; restores 'Eliphelet son of Ahasbai' behind the truncated 'Eliphal son of Ur.'

Ellicott suggests 'Hepher the Maachathite' behind the unknown 'Mecherathite,' and reconstructs 'Eliam son of Ahithophel the Gilonite' behind 'Ahijah the Pelonite' — the traitor's name perhaps veiled or lost.

Ellicott records the wavering of 'Hezro/Hezri,' places him at Carmel south of Hebron, and sets 'Naarai son of Ezbai' beside Samuel's 'Paarai the Arbite' — Carmel and Arab close together in southern Judah.
Ellicott restores 'Igal son of Nathan of Zobah' and, behind 'Mibhar son of Haggeri,' reads 'of Zobah' and 'the Gadite,' with Samuel's lost 'Bani the Gadite' — the list reaching into Syria and Gilead.
Ellicott ranks Zelek the Ammonite among David's many alien warriors (Uriah, Ittai, Ithmah), and places Naharai, Joab's armour-bearer, at Beeroth in Benjamin.
Matthew Henry reads the Ammonite (with the Moabite of v.46) against Deut. 23:3 — the law dispensed for proven loyalty, a token that the Son of David would have his worthies among the Gentiles.
Ellicott traces the two Ithrites to the clan of Jether of Kirjath-jearim; Keil and Delitzsch fix the character of this third class — no deeds related, honoured simply by enrolment in the royal guard.

Ellicott marks Uriah the Hittite enrolled without his dark history (left to 2 Samuel 11); Samuel's list closes here with 'all, thirty and seven,' the following names a later revision unique to Chronicles.
Wesley: Uriah is the last of Samuel's catalogue; the added names are men of valour not of the original thirty.
Ellicott reads 'a head, and thirty with him,' reckoning from David's guard of six hundred that Adina's corps was larger than usual — the roll now crossing into Reuben.
Wesley: the 'thirty' were thirty captains under Adina as their colonel.

Ellicott owns the lost form of 'the Mithnite' (Septuagint and Syriac diverge); Keil and Delitzsch place the pair among the sixteen added eastern names of the subordinate third class, deeds nowhere told.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott together map the pair onto the trans-Jordan — Ashtaroth in Bashan, Aroer in Reuben or Gad — correcting 'Jehiel/Jeuel' and the misprinted father's name.
Keil and Delitzsch keep the brothers among the added eastern names; Ellicott suggests Jediael may be the Manassite who came to David at Ziklag — an early loyalty later honoured.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read 'the Mahavite' as 'the Mahanaimite' (Mahanaim in Gad), keeping the sons of Elnaam in the eastern land, and carry forward Ithmah the Moabite.
Matthew Henry reads the Moabite among the mighty (against Deut. 23:3) as a pledge that the Son of David would have his worthies among the Gentiles.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott both find 'the Mesobaite' corrupt — Bertheau linking it to Samuel's 'Mezobaite,' Ellicott guessing 'of Zobah' — the roll's last name defeating certain reading, yet still enrolled for honour.

themes: from the famous to the forgotten
